title: Reading and Use of English – Part 7
description: You are going to read an article about the history of the post office. Choose from paragraphs A–G which fits each gap. There is one extra paragraph.

content.articleTitle: The Universal Link: A History of the Mail
content.text: The postal service is one of the oldest and most essential forms of human communication. In the ancient world, kings and emperors maintained elaborate networks of messengers to carry decrees to the edges of their empires. These systems, like the Persian *Angarium*, relied on a series of relay stations where riders could change horses and continue at full speed. ||(41)|| [41]However, these early services were reserved strictly for the state[/41]. For the common person, sending a message required hiring a private traveler or a merchant. This began to change in the 17th century with the establishment of public post offices. Initially, the system was complex and expensive, as the cost of delivery was calculated by the distance traveled and the number of sheets of paper. ||(42)|| [42]This 'receiver-pays' model was a disaster for the poor[/42]. If a person couldn't afford the postage, the letter was simply returned or destroyed. The solution came in 1840 from Rowland Hill, a British reformer who proposed a 'Uniform Penny Post.' For the price of one penny, a letter could be sent anywhere in the country, regardless of distance. ||(43)|| [43]The 'Penny Black,' the world’s first adhesive postage stamp, was the symbol of this revolution[/43]. It turned the post office into a mass-market utility, sparking a surge in literacy and personal correspondence. Families could now stay in touch across great distances, and businesses could market their products to a national audience. ||(44)|| [44]As the 19th century progressed, the post office became the engine of global trade[/44]. The 'Pony Express' in America and the 'Royal Mail' steamships in Britain connected the far reaches of the world. This era of physical mail reached its peak with the invention of the 'Airmail' service, which used early airplanes to cross oceans in days rather than weeks. ||(45)|| [45]However, the digital revolution of the late 20th century posed a fatal threat to the traditional letter[/45]. The arrival of email and instant messaging meant that information could be sent for free and in real-time. The 'Snail Mail' service, as it came to be called, struggled to remain relevant in a world where physical paper felt like a relic of the past. ||(46)|| [46]Today, while letter volumes continue to decline, the post office is experiencing an unexpected rebirth through e-commerce[/46]. The rise of online shopping has turned the mail service into a massive logistics operation for parcels and packages. The institution that began as a relay for royal decrees is now the backbone of the global digital marketplace.

paragraphs.B: Indeed, [41] these royal networks [/41] were marvels of efficiency. A message could travel across thousands of miles in a fraction of the time it would take a normal traveler, ensuring that the central government remained in control of its distant territories.
paragraphs.F: Crucially, [42] the postage was paid by the recipient [/42], not the sender. This meant that the mail carrier had to wait at the door while the person counted their coins, a process that was both slow and financially risky for the post office.
paragraphs.C: This [43] radical idea [/43] shifted the cost of postage to the sender. By making it cheap and prepaid, Hill ensured that the post office could process letters much faster, as carriers no longer had to collect money at the doorstep.
paragraphs.D: The [44] social impact of this change [/44] was immediate. The number of letters sent in Britain doubled within a year, leading to the creation of the iconic red pillar boxes that became a permanent fixture of the urban landscape.
paragraphs.G: This [45] speed of delivery [/45] was essential for the growing colonial empires. It allowed for the synchronization of international markets and the rapid spread of news, making the world feel like a much smaller and more interconnected place.
paragraphs.A: This [46] shift in communication habits [/46] forced many national postal services to cut costs and close local branches. They had to reinvent themselves, moving away from the delivery of personal letters toward a focus on business and government services.
paragraphs.E: Many [47] modern collectors [/47] still prize the 'Penny Black' for its historical significance. They argue that despite the rise of digital communication, the physical beauty and tangibility of a stamp represent a lost art form of the industrial age.

questions.explanation: B expands on the 'royal networks' mentioned before the gap and explains their efficiency in controlling territories.
F explains the 'receiver-pays' model, which fits the text's description of the system being complex and risky for the poor.
C identifies Hill's 'radical idea' (prepaid postage) as the solution to the problems of the receiver-pays model.
D describes the 'social impact' of the Penny Post (doubling letter volumes) and the introduction of pillar boxes.
G links the 'speed of delivery' from Airmail to the needs of 'colonial empires' and 'international markets'.
A refers to the 'shift in communication habits' caused by the digital revolution and the resulting need for post offices to reinvent themselves.
